title: Assessment: Ambient Access and Hidden Dependencies

questions.question: A Registry provides convenience by allowing any object to look up services by name. What is the primary design cost introduced by this convenience?

options.A: It creates construction coupling because objects must inherit from the registry
options.B: It makes dependencies implicit — callers cannot see what an object depends on without reading its internals
options.C: It introduces a boundary mismatch between the domain model and the registry schema
options.D: It prevents unit testing because registries require real implementations

questions.explanation: The fundamental Registry cost is hidden dependencies — the dependency graph is invisible from the outside. A is wrong (no inheritance required). C conflates Registry with ORM concerns. D is partly true but the root design cost is the visibility problem, not testability per se.

questions.question: Under what narrow condition is a Registry legitimately justified over explicit dependency injection?

options.A: When the team prefers not to refactor the composition root
options.B: When the dependency is genuinely global in scope and passing it through many layers would create significant overhead
options.C: Whenever more than five services need access to the same object
options.D: When the dependency changes at runtime and must be hot-swapped

questions.explanation: The narrow justification is: genuinely global scope + measurably high injection overhead. Team preference (A) and service count (C) are not design pressures. Hot-swapping (D) is a runtime variation concern addressed by Strategy or Plugin, not Registry.

questions.question: Service Locator is sometimes called a legitimate tool. In which context is its use most defensible?

options.A: Any application where the team wants to avoid writing a composition root
options.B: A framework or plugin host where the caller does not control object construction
options.C: Large codebases where there are more than 50 injectable services
options.D: Systems where all services have the same lifecycle (request-scoped)

questions.explanation: Service Locator is most defensible when the caller genuinely cannot receive the dependency by injection — e.g., a framework instantiates the object and the developer has no access to the constructor call. The other options describe situations where DI is typically still viable.

questions.question: Contradiction 1 states the threshold between acceptable Service Locator and architectural debt is unresolved. What makes this particular contradiction unresolvable by the research reviewed in this course?

options.A: Service Locator was deprecated after GoF published their book
options.B: The threshold depends on context factors (team size, framework, call-tree depth) that no study has empirically measured
options.C: Fowler and Nystrom use different definitions of Service Locator, making comparison impossible
options.D: There are no real-world codebases that use Service Locator anymore

questions.explanation: The research surveyed by this course found no empirical measurement of the point where SL transitions from acceptable convenience to architectural debt. The contextual factors vary too much for a universal rule. A, C, D are factually incorrect.

questions.question: What is the most concrete indicator that a Singleton is being used as ambient global state rather than as a legitimate single-instance controller?

options.A: The singleton class has more than one method
options.B: The singleton is referenced in more than 10 places
options.C: Test isolation breaks — state leaks from one test to the next unless explicitly reset
options.D: The singleton was created before DI containers were available

questions.explanation: State leaking between tests is the most concrete operational signal that the singleton is carrying global mutable state rather than controlling a genuine single-instance lifecycle. Reference count (B) and method count (A) are not indicators. Historical origin (D) is irrelevant.

questions.question: Distinguish a legitimate Singleton from Singleton-as-global-access-point.

options.A: Legitimate Singleton is thread-safe; global access point is not
options.B: Legitimate Singleton enforces a genuine single-instance domain requirement; global access point exists only for convenience of avoiding injection
options.C: Legitimate Singleton is created at startup; global access point is created lazily
options.D: Legitimate Singleton implements an interface; global access point returns a concrete class

questions.explanation: The distinction is whether the 'single instance' constraint is a genuine domain requirement (e.g., one printer spooler) or just a convenience for avoiding DI overhead. Thread safety (A) and creation timing (C) are implementation details, not the distinguishing criterion. Interface usage (D) is unrelated.

questions.question: Claim C5 identifies the central failure mode for patterns as applying them before real pressure exists. Which of the following best applies this claim to the Singleton pattern?

options.A: Using Singleton for a configuration store that is genuinely read-only and process-global
options.B: Using Singleton for every service class to ensure one instance per application
options.C: Using Singleton to enforce that a connection pool has exactly one instance managing thread-safe access
options.D: Using Singleton for a hardware interface driver where one physical device must map to one software object

questions.explanation: Applying Singleton to every service 'for consistency' is using it before any single-instance pressure is real — it's cargo-cult architecture. A, C, and D all have genuine single-instance requirements that justify the pattern.

questions.question: Explain why dependency visibility is described as a 'first-class design concern' in this module. Give one example where low visibility created a concrete problem.

required_points: Dependency visibility means the set of things an object depends on is declared and visible to callers — not hidden inside the implementation
Low visibility means callers cannot reason about what an object needs without reading its source, which makes testing, debugging, and refactoring harder
Concrete example: e.g., an object using a Registry internally — a test can call the method without knowing it needs a DB connection until a runtime error occurs
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Example can involve Singleton or Service Locator as the hidden-dependency mechanism
answer_key.full_credit: Defines visibility, explains why it matters, gives a concrete failure-mode example
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct definition or correct example but not both with the causal link

questions.question: Describe the test isolation problem caused by Singleton global state and explain one way to resolve it without eliminating the Singleton.

required_points: Problem: singleton carries mutable state across test boundaries — test A modifies the singleton and test B inherits that modification, making tests order-dependent

Resolution without elimination: provide a reset/clear method that tests call in setUp/tearDown, OR scope the singleton to the test context, OR use a DI approach that injects the singleton as an interface (tests inject a fresh instance)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any valid test isolation strategy that addresses singleton state leakage
answer_key.full_credit: Correct diagnosis of the test-isolation failure mode and one concrete resolution strategy
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct diagnosis without a resolution strategy

questions.prompt: A senior developer on your team argues: 'Service Locator, Registry, and Singleton are all just convenient global access — they're all bad and should be eliminated.' A junior developer argues they're all perfectly fine and easier than DI. Using Contradiction 1 and the ambient access analysis from Module 3, write a nuanced position that neither fully agrees with either developer.
questions.guidance: A strong response acknowledges the legitimate use cases for all three (framework constraints, genuine single-instance requirements, genuinely global infrastructure objects) while explaining their shared visibility cost and the specific conditions that justify them. It should cite the unresolved threshold in Contradiction 1 and avoid universal rules in either direction.

rubric.A: Acknowledges legitimate use cases for all three, articulates the shared visibility cost, cites the unresolved threshold, gives specific contextual factors that shift the judgment
rubric.B: Correct on the visibility cost and at least one legitimate use case, limited on contextual nuance or Contradiction 1
rubric.C: Picks a side (agrees with senior or junior) without contextual differentiation
